bugs.error_message: Wrong reference: fmtDate should be formatDateTimeCsv
User: 'the time captured in the invoices is incorrect it could be because it's not adding +3 for bahrain time zone'
bugs.root_cause: Display-only bug; storage was already correct. Verified: Postgres TimeZone=GMT, receipts.created_at is 'timestamp without time zone', and JS now() vs PG now() drift measured 0.8s — so timestamps are stored in clean UTC. The pages (contacts/receipts/raffle) are SERVER components, so Date.toLocaleString() with no explicit zone formats in the SERVER's timezone, which is UTC on Vercel. Every invoice therefore rendered 3 hours early in production. It looked correct in local dev ONLY because the dev machine's zone is Asia/Bahrain, so the bug could never reproduce locally. The CSV export shared the fault via toISOString() (UTC by definition), and the export filename used toISOString().slice(0,10), which stamps yesterday's date between 00:00-03:00 Bahrain.
bugs.fix: Added lib/datetime.ts exposing formatDateTime / formatDateTimeCsv / todayInBahrain, all passing timeZone:'Asia/Bahrain' explicitly to module-level Intl.DateTimeFormat instances (hourCycle:'h23' so midnight renders 00:00, not 24:00). Replaced every toLocaleString()/toISOString() in app/contacts/page.tsx, app/receipts/page.tsx, app/raffle/page.tsx and app/api/export/route.ts; deleted the now-redundant fmtDate helper. CSV headers relabelled 'Timestamp (Bahrain)' / 'Last Seen (Bahrain)' since cells are no longer ISO/UTC. No data migration required. Verified by running the real formatter against real DB rows under TZ=UTC: IN-000545 went from '7/15/2026, 11:35:11 AM' to '15/07/2026, 14:35'. 9 unit tests in lib/datetime.test.ts pass identically under TZ=UTC, TZ=Asia/Bahrain and TZ=America/New_York.

bugs.error_message: Wrong reference: messageFor should be signInErrorMessage
Security audit finding #2: sign-in had no brute-force protection — unlimited bcrypt.compare attempts per second, no lockout, no rate limit, and no log that an attack was happening.
bugs.root_cause: authorize() in auth.ts looked up the user and compared the password with no attempt counter of any kind. A public portal + password auth + no lockout = credential stuffing works and leaves no trace. Two related holes in the same function: an unknown email returned before bcrypt ran, so response time (~900ms vs instant) enumerated the staff list (audit finding #9); and the bcrypt cost 10 was copy-pasted at three call sites (auth.ts, admin/users/actions.ts, prisma/seed.ts) so it could drift.
bugs.fix: Added login_attempts table (migration 0003) counting failures per email and per IP. lib/login-throttle.ts holds the pure policy (email: 5 failures → lock, doubling 1→2→4→8→15 min cap; IP: 30 failures, deliberately loose because a shop's cashiers share one NAT and locking the store mid-shift stops them scanning). lib/login-attempts.ts holds the Postgres reads/writes plus opportunistic GC. auth.ts throws a CredentialsSignin subclass whose code carries the wait ('too_many_attempts:60'), which the sign-in form renders as a human message. Enumeration closed via lib/password.ts ABSENT_USER_HASH — a bcrypt hash of an unrecorded random value, compared against when the email is unknown so the timing matches. BCRYPT_COST now defined once and shared. Failed sign-ins are console.warn'd.

bugs.root_cause: Property access on potentially null/undefined value
bugs.fix: Added null safety (optional chaining or null check) | Also: * Identify which candidate signed the body, or null if none ; * Returns the candidate rather than a boolean on purpose. Ea

bugs.error_message: Security audit finding #3: the webhook failed OPEN — if no app secret could be resolved, signature verification silently switched off and /api/webhook accepted anything from anyone.
bugs.root_cause: lib/webhook.ts verifySignatureAgainstAny() began `if (appSecrets.length === 0) return true` — documented and unit-tested as 'verification off'. getAppSecrets() built that list from META_APP_SECRET plus each store's decrypted secret, and a decrypt failure only logged and continued. So a wrong/rotated ENCRYPTION_KEY, or a restored DB, emptied the list and turned verification off — precisely when it mattered most. The insecure state was indistinguishable from the secure one in the logs.
bugs.fix: Removed the fail-open entirely; there is no 'no secrets = allow' path and no insecure escape hatch. The route now resolves candidate secrets for the phone_number_id the payload names: none resolvable -> 503 (our misconfiguration, so Meta retries and the events can still land once Settings/ENCRYPTION_KEY are fixed); signature mismatch -> 403. Decrypt failure logs loudly and yields no candidate rather than skipping verification. Every rejection names what to fix.

bugs.error_message: Security audit finding #4: one store could forge the other store's WhatsApp delivery statuses. The two stores are legally separate companies sharing one webhook URL.
bugs.root_cause: verifySignatureAgainstAny() returned a BOOLEAN — it discarded which secret matched, which is exactly the fact needed to scope the events. A payload signed by ANY store's app was accepted, then applyStatusEvent() looked the receipt up by wamid ALONE (db.receipt.findFirst({ where: { wamid } })) with no store scoping. Each store's manager sets their own app secret in Settings, so Morslon could sign a body carrying Modern Sources' wamids and rewrite their receipts.
bugs.fix: Replaced verifySignatureAgainstAny with findSigner(), which returns the matching candidate rather than a boolean. The route resolves the store from metadata.phone_number_id (safe to read unverified: it only picks WHICH secret to check, and a lie fails verification), verifies against that store's secret only, then applies two layers: (1) any change whose phone_number_id != the signer's is refused as cross-store, blocking a mixed body signed by one app; (2) the receipt lookup is scoped { wamid, storeId: signer.store.id }, so a wamid alone is not proof of ownership. logIncomingMessage now uses the signing store instead of a second phone lookup. CAVEAT: while two stores share the META_APP_SECRET env fallback they can still sign for each other — per-store secrets in Settings are what make the isolation real.
bugs.fix_verified: Sent a real morslon-signed body smuggling a change for another phone_number_id carrying receipt IN-00087's real wamid marked 'failed' (failed always wins over 'read', so it would have been visible). IN-00087 stayed 'read' with no error. Also confirmed on real data that the same wamid resolves under morslon but is not found under modern-sources.
